Glossary About
Nineteenth Century

Carl Georg Lange

1834 – 1900

Emotion, physiology, and the bodily constitution of felt life.

I take “Carl Lange” here to mean Carl Georg Lange (1834–1900), the Danish physician, neurologist, and professor of pathological anatomy at the University of Copenhagen, not Friedrich Albert Lange. The relevant corpus is not limited to the famous “James–Lange theory” of emotion. The central texts are Lange’s 1885 Danish work Om Sindsbevægelser; its German, French, and English transmission; his 1886 work on periodical depressions; and his 1899 Danish book on pleasure and aesthetics, Bidrag til Nydelsernes Fysiologi som Grundlag for en rationel Æstetik. Lange’s reputation in English has been filtered heavily through William James, but Danish, German, and French materials show a broader and more philosophically ambitious project.

My main reconstruction is this: Lange’s philosophy of mind is an embodied, anti-dualist, psychophysiological naturalism of affect. He does not simply say that “the body causes emotion” in a crude materialist sense. More precisely, he denies that emotion should be explained by positing a prior immaterial mental event, a “soul-force,” which then pushes the body into expression. For Lange, the emotional life of consciousness is the organism’s own physiological life becoming felt, interpreted, remembered, symbolized, and culturally shaped. The “soul,” if the term is retained, should name the integrated psychophysical person; if it is treated as a separate causal substance that explains bodily changes, Lange’s theory rejects it.

1. The problem of terminology: soul, mind, consciousness, affect

A rigorous reading of Lange has to begin with translation. The title Om Sindsbevægelser is usually rendered as On Emotions or The Emotions, but the Danish word Sind does not map cleanly onto one modern English word. It can point toward mind, mood, inwardness, disposition, or the seat of affect. The German title Ueber Gemüthsbewegungen brings in Gemüth, another difficult word, often lying somewhere between mind, heart, temperament, and affective interiority. Claudia Wassmann has stressed that nineteenth-century German psychological vocabulary—Gemüth, Seele, Geist, Bewusstsein—was often used in ways that do not correspond neatly to later English distinctions among mind, soul, spirit, and consciousness.

This matters because Lange’s argument is easy to distort. If one translates everything into the modern English “emotion,” one may miss that he is intervening in older debates about affections of the soul, passions, moods, temperaments, spiritual feeling, and conscious experience. His book was translated from Danish into German in 1887, French in 1895, and English only in 1922, and the English translation was made from the German version while also being compared with the French. That transmission history helps explain why the Anglophone reception often assimilated Lange to James instead of reading him as a Danish medical-philosophical author with his own conceptual field.

A further difficulty is that Lange distinguishes, though not always consistently, between simple affects or emotional movements and more complex emotional conditions such as love, envy, admiration, religious exaltation, and aesthetic pleasure. Wassmann notes that Lange used Affect for simpler emotion and separated these from more complex affective conditions. This is crucial: Lange’s physiology is not meant only to describe a few reflexive episodes such as fear or anger; it becomes, in his later aesthetics, a general theory of how bodily life, memory, symbolism, culture, and valuation generate consciousness of pleasure and displeasure.

2. Lange’s negative project: against the “soul” as an occult causal force

Lange’s starting point is methodological. He observes that both ordinary opinion and much scientific psychology treat an emotion as though it were a distinct inner entity: a psychic state, power, or substance that first arises in the soul and then produces bodily manifestations. He thinks this is a pseudo-explanation. It gives a name to the phenomenon and then treats the name as a cause. Popular psychology imagines emotions almost like inner forces or demons acting upon the organism; learned psychology, in his view, often repeats the same pattern in more respectable language.

His preferred question is not: “How does a mental emotion cause bodily symptoms?” It is: What if what we call emotion consists in those bodily-functional disturbances as they become conscious? Lange says every emotion has tangible factors: a cause, usually a sensory impression modified by memory or association, and an effect, namely changes in bodily functions, especially vasomotor and circulatory changes. The controversial issue is whether one must posit a further psychic entity between these two. Lange’s answer is mostly no.

This is why his theory is anti-dualist in method. He does not begin with a metaphysical proof that the soul does not exist. Instead, he argues that the explanatory appeal to a hidden psychic cause is unnecessary and unscientific where physiological mechanisms can do the work. When people say that bodily symptoms are “caused by the soul,” they are often appealing to precisely what needs explanation. A contemporary Danish review by Harald Høffding captured Lange’s point well: the ordinary claim that body-phenomena are caused by the soul rests on an unfounded distinction between soul and body and is not directly provable as science.

But Lange’s anti-dualism should not be flattened into eliminativism. He does not deny lived grief, fear, joy, admiration, or religious exaltation. He denies that their depth or purity depends on their being generated by a separate immaterial faculty. His famous example of the mourning mother is decisive: grief is not made shallow or false by being understood physiologically. Its reality is not diminished if we say that it cannot exist without bodily attributes.

So Lange’s view of “soul” is best reconstructed as follows. The soul is not an independent inner agent that first feels and then makes the body tremble, blush, pale, sweat, or weep. The soul, insofar as the term remains meaningful, is the lived unity of bodily, affective, cognitive, and conscious life. He rejects the soul as a separate causal posit; he does not thereby abolish interior life.

3. The positive doctrine: emotion as embodied psychophysiological event

Lange’s best-known positive thesis is that emotions depend on bodily changes, especially changes in the vasomotor system: alterations in the distribution and pressure of blood, vascular contraction and dilation, and related organic modifications. He argues that the causes of emotion converge on the vasomotor center, and that stimulation of this system is fundamental to affective experience.

The simplest version of the thesis is: sensory impression → vasomotor change → conscious emotion. But Lange’s actual account is more sophisticated. The path to bodily change may be direct, as in immediate sensory stimulation, or indirect, passing through memory, association, imagination, and cortical processing. A loaded gun and an empty gun may present similar sensory appearances, but the emotional response depends on what is known, remembered, and associated with the perception. Likewise, a child’s reaction to medicine can be learned: after prior experience, the sight of the spoon can trigger the bodily-affective process before the taste is present.

This point is philosophically important. Lange is not a simple stimulus-response reductionist. He makes cognition and memory part of the causal route into emotion. A perception becomes affectively significant when it is embedded in a history of learned associations and representations. Thus, the organism does not merely react to physical stimuli; it reacts to meaningful objects, where meaning is acquired through memory, expectation, habit, and interpretation.

He also resists a strict boundary between “material” and “mental” causes. The same emotional physiology may be reached through bodily, sensory, mnemonic, or ideational routes. In The Emotions he argues that so-called material and mental emotions are not physiologically different in essence; both require vasomotor stimulation, though the paths toward that stimulation may differ. In his later work on pleasure he again says that memories, thoughts, and associations may influence blood flow and mood more strongly and lastingly than simple sensations, and that no definite boundary can be drawn between mental or “spiritual” and material causes of emotional phenomena.

This gives Lange a layered model:

First, there is an external or internal occasion: a sound, sight, memory, word, thought, religious image, bodily condition, toxin, disease process, or social situation.

Second, this occasion activates nervous and organic processes, especially involuntary and vasomotor functions.

Third, the organism becomes conscious of these bodily changes as a distinctive affective state.

Fourth, the state is interpreted under a concept or narrative: fear of the gun, grief for the dead, religious ecstasy, aesthetic admiration, inexplicable depression.

Fifth, the interpretation may feed back into the bodily system, intensifying or modifying the state.

That final feedback point is important even where Lange himself emphasizes the body more than later philosophers might prefer. Høffding’s critique asks whether Lange should distinguish two stages: a brain-to-body movement and then a body-to-brain return that becomes conscious as emotion. This is not a rejection of Lange’s insight, but a refinement of its architecture.

4. Consciousness: affective experience is bodily self-awareness, but not merely raw sensation

Lange’s account of consciousness begins from a denial of introspective certainty. He argues that we have no immediate means of sharply separating a “mental” sensation from a “physical” one. The claim that something belongs purely to the mind is already a theory, not a datum given directly in consciousness.

This is one of the deepest philosophical claims in his work. Consciousness does not transparently reveal its own ontological basis. A person may say, “I am sad,” “I am afraid,” or “I am exalted,” but that report does not prove that sadness, fear, or exaltation first existed as a disembodied mental event. Consciousness gives us the experience; it does not automatically give us the causal structure of the experience.

Lange repeatedly appeals to cases where bodily or pathological changes alter emotional consciousness. Alcohol, hashish, disease, and bodily states can induce, suppress, or transform affect. Pathology may produce depression, rage, terror, or liveliness without adequate external motive; the patient may even recognize that the feeling is groundless.

His 1886 work on periodical depressions develops this insight clinically. He describes patients with heaviness, exhaustion, apathy, mental stiffness, paralysis-like difficulty, lack of spirits, mental emptiness, anxiety, and misery. He distinguishes this from ordinary sorrow by the lack or inadequacy of a psychological basis: patients may know that their misery and anxiety are groundless and yet still suffer them.

This is central for extracting a philosophy of mind. Lange separates affective consciousness from justifying reasons. A mood can be conscious, intense, and existentially overwhelming even when the subject judges that there is no adequate reason for it. The affect is not identical with a belief. Nor is it reducible to an explicit judgment. It is a bodily-conscious condition that may or may not be rationally integrated into the subject’s understanding of the world.

That gives Lange a surprisingly modern-looking account of mood. Emotion may be object-directed: fear of a loaded gun, grief over a death, joy at a reunion. Mood may be objectless or underdetermined: depression, anxiety, irritability, exaltation, or dullness without an adequate object. In both cases, consciousness is embodied. But in the first case, the bodily-affective state is organized around an intelligible object; in the second, the body generates a field of feeling for which cognition may search after the fact.

5. Mind, memory, and meaning: Lange is not a crude reductionist

A common misunderstanding is that Lange reduces mind to vascular motion. The better reading is subtler: he treats mind as a set of representational and associative routes by which the organism’s bodily-affective systems are activated, organized, and interpreted.

Memory is especially important. Lange notes that memory can call forth emotion without present sensation, because earlier processes leave persistent traces in the brain. This means that past experience alters the organism’s future affective susceptibility. A remembered insult, a religious hymn, an image of a dead loved one, or a learned danger can become a real physiological-affective cause.

Here the “mental” is not illusory. It is causally real, but not as a ghostly substance. Thoughts, memories, and associations are embodied neural processes with the power to route energy into affective bodily systems. Lange’s model therefore permits a robust account of symbolic and cultural life. A word, for example, is not emotionally powerful merely as a sound. It works because it activates meanings, memories, associations, and bodily dispositions.

This becomes explicit in the aesthetics text. Lange argues that poetry and words require knowledge of lexical meaning and stored impressions. A religious hymn can produce ecstatic mood only in those with the relevant religious presuppositions and stored impressions; a love poem similarly requires affective “strings” already present in the hearer.

The result is a philosophy of mind in which meaning is neither purely intellectual nor merely physiological. Meaning is the organization of bodily responsiveness by memory, language, practice, and belief. A sign becomes affective when it can mobilize the body through the subject’s acquired history.

6. Emotion categories: conventional names for continuous psychophysical variation

Lange is skeptical of ordinary emotion-taxonomies. He argues that named emotions—fear, anger, sorrow, joy, admiration, and so on—do not exhaust the real continuity of affective life. They are practical and conventional groupings, not necessarily natural essences. Scientific work, he thinks, should investigate the underlying physiological reactions rather than assume that ordinary language has already divided the field correctly.

This is an important anticipation of later debates. On Lange’s view, emotion concepts are interpretive overlays on continuous bodily-affective processes. The same basic bodily systems may produce many shades of experience depending on intensity, duration, cause, memory, object, and cultural framing. This helps explain why he is interested not only in fear and anger but also in depression, religious ecstasy, aesthetic pleasure, admiration, and the enjoyment of poetry.

The categories are not meaningless; they are phenomenologically and culturally useful. But they are not the final ontology of mind. They are names we give to regions of a fluid psychophysiological field.

7. Pleasure and aesthetics: from emotion theory to a general theory of value

Lange’s 1899 book on pleasure and aesthetics extends his philosophy of mind far beyond the usual “James–Lange” story. The Danish source is machine-read OCR and explicitly warns that the text may contain errors, so some caution is needed. Still, the legible passages show a clear extension of his psychophysiological method into aesthetics, pleasure, religious feeling, and artistic experience.

Lange begins from the instability of pleasure. What gives pleasure differs by person and time; even sorrow, fear, anger, and moods can sometimes be enjoyed under certain conditions. He provisionally defines pleasure not as a simple inner quality but as a state or mood one strives to enter or remain in. This makes pleasure functional and organismic rather than merely introspective.

This is a major philosophical move. Pleasure is not a homogeneous mental substance. It is a relation between an organism and a state it seeks, sustains, repeats, or cultivates. The same affective tone may be painful in one context and pleasurable in another: tragedy, religious penitence, theatrical terror, melancholic music, disciplined exertion, aesthetic admiration, and ecstatic worship all become intelligible within this framework.

Lange’s aesthetics also treats art as an affective technology. Artistic pleasure may come from sensation, sympathy, rhythm, representation, or admiration. The Stanford Encyclopedia discussion of the ontology of art notes Lange’s claim that pleasures have sources including variety, sympathy, and admiration of the artist’s overcoming of difficulties; admiration depends on awareness of origins, which is why an original and an indiscernible copy may differ aesthetically.

This confirms that Lange is not merely interested in “bodily feelings” detached from culture. Aesthetic pleasure requires knowledge. The connoisseur’s pleasure may depend on awareness of artistic difficulty, technique, originality, provenance, and performance. In theater, for example, the spectator’s enjoyment is not only illusion; it may be admiration for the actor’s art. In ballet, pleasure may arise partly from admiration at what bodies can do.

Thus Lange’s mature view can be formulated as follows: aesthetic consciousness is embodied pleasure mediated by cultivated perception, memory, comparison, and admiration. The body is still central, but it is a historically educated body.

8. Religion and theology: not a distraction, but a test case for the theory

Lange’s theological comments are not incidental. They show how far he intended his theory to reach. Religious emotion, ecstasy, hymnody, ritual movement, stigmata, and theological physiology all appear as important cases of psychophysical life.

In The Emotions, Lange discusses Malebranche and finds in him something like an early vasomotor theory. Malebranche had spoken of the “essence of life” around the arteries; Lange translates this into modern physiological language as a claim that strong emotional impressions produce vasomotor innervation and circulatory disturbance. He regards this as remarkable because it makes circulatory disturbance primary among the bodily concomitants of emotion.

This is not merely historical trivia. Malebranche is a theologian-philosopher of occasionalism, grace, and divine order. Lange’s interest in him shows that theological psychology had already tried to think the relation between soul, body, passion, and circulation. Lange does not dismiss that tradition as nonsense; he redescribes it physiologically. Theological language becomes a historical precursor to physiological psychology.

Lange also cites Johann Jakob Engel’s statement that man has only one soul and suggests that the popular conception imagines affections almost like demons that disturb the soul. This is one of his clearest criticisms of older soul-language: the problem is not that people speak of the soul, but that they hypostatize emotions as invaders of an otherwise unified inner substance.

Spinoza is, for Lange, the closest philosophical predecessor. Lange praises Spinoza for placing bodily phenomena and mental activity on the same plane and for treating affect as involving both bodily affections and ideas. This matters enormously. Lange’s position is not best described as Cartesian materialism. It is closer to a Spinozist psychophysical monism or parallelism: mind and body are not two separate theaters causally pushing one another from outside; affect is one event viewed through bodily and conscious aspects.

His treatment of religious ecstasy in the 1899 aesthetics book is especially important. He describes ecstatic joy over divinity, heavenly joy, angelic singing, visions of heaven, but also the deepest pain and anxiety over Christ’s wounds or hell. He links such states to abnormal mood and bodily conditions, including phenomena such as stigmata and bloody sweat. He also suggests that dance in religious ceremonies may help promote ecstatic states already grounded in religious mood.

One must be careful here. Lange is not doing theology in the confessional sense. He is not proving or disproving God, grace, immortality, revelation, or the metaphysical existence of the soul. But he is doing something philosophically serious: he is showing that religious consciousness is embodied, memory-laden, symbolically mediated, ritually intensified, and physiologically real.

A religious hymn does not produce ecstasy merely by acoustic stimulus. It produces ecstasy in those whose religious memories, concepts, expectations, and dispositions allow the words and music to mobilize the affective body. Religious experience, then, is neither “merely subjective” nor merely “supernatural” in Lange’s explanatory frame. It is a psychophysical event in which doctrine, memory, symbol, ritual, and bodily excitation converge.

There was also immediate Danish concern over the implications for the soul. I found a contemporaneous Danish response by Morten Pontoppidan titled Menneskesjælens Tilværelse. I Anledning af C. Langes Bog om Sindsbevægelser—roughly, “The Existence of the Human Soul: Occasioned by C. Lange’s Book on Emotions.” I could identify the work bibliographically, but not securely access enough of the text to reconstruct its argument. Its title alone, however, confirms that Lange’s book was immediately read in Denmark as bearing on the existence and status of the human soul.

9. Reason, education, and self-control

Lange’s account of reason is also embodied. He suggests that intellectual operations affect cerebral blood supply and that education may be understood as training higher reflexes to control lower emotional vascular reflexes. In that sense, rational self-mastery is not the triumph of an immaterial faculty over the body. It is the body-brain system acquiring regulatory habits.

This yields a striking picture of moral psychology. “Reason” is not an autonomous sovereign outside affect. It is a cultivated organization of the organism. Self-control is a learned capacity by which some psychophysical circuits inhibit, redirect, or regulate others. Lange can therefore explain moral and intellectual development without abandoning his physiological framework.

At the same time, this part of Lange’s text contains dated nineteenth-century assumptions, including hierarchical cultural and social claims that should not be carried over. The philosophically salvageable thesis is not any ranking of peoples or classes; it is the more general idea that rational conduct depends on embodied regulation, education, habit, and inhibition.

10. Depression and pathological mood: affect without adequate object

Lange’s work on periodical depressions deserves attention because it deepens his philosophy of consciousness. In the 1886 lecture, he emphasizes the frequency and seriousness of such conditions and reports broad clinical experience. He describes depression as involving both mental and physical exhaustion, heaviness, apathy, lack of joy, and a kind of mental paralysis or stiffness.

The crucial philosophical point is his distinction between depression and ordinary sorrow. Sorrow is typically intelligible through its object: loss, misfortune, disappointment. Periodical depression may present similar misery, anxiety, tears, and emptiness, but without an adequate psychological basis. The patient may know that the feeling is groundless.

This supports three major claims.

First, consciousness can be affectively saturated without being rationally justified. One may feel catastrophe without believing, on reflection, that catastrophe has occurred.

Second, bodily pathology can shape the intentional field of consciousness. The world appears heavy, empty, threatening, or meaningless not necessarily because of a belief but because the organism’s mood-state structures the field of possible meaning.

Third, the mind is not reducible to explicit cognition. Affective consciousness has its own inertia, tempo, and bodily ground.

Lange’s own uric-acid hypothesis should not be treated as a present-day medical conclusion. Even in his own discussion, he notes that the uric-acid facts by themselves do not directly establish pathogenesis, though he thought they could guide treatment. The enduring philosophical value lies elsewhere: he recognized that mood can be bodily generated, consciously overwhelming, and reflectively judged as groundless.

11. Reception: Høffding, French physiology, translation, and the James problem

The standard Anglophone label “James–Lange theory” is historically convenient but philosophically flattening. Modern scholarship has argued that this composite label can be misleading because James and Lange had different emphases, and because Lange’s broader work on aesthetics and pleasure does not simply duplicate James.

Høffding’s Danish review is one of the most valuable early interpretations. He recognizes Lange’s central claim: emotion is not one thing and bodily effect another; from the physiological standpoint, the bodily effects constitute the emotion. But Høffding also criticizes Lange for overvaluing the physiological perspective. Without subjective experience, Høffding argues, a clinician could not see sorrow merely in physiological phenomena. This is a strong objection: physiology alone may identify bodily changes, but the meaning “sorrow” requires the subject’s lived perspective.

That critique helps improve Lange’s philosophy of mind. The best Langean view should not say, “Emotion is only vascular disturbance.” It should say: emotion is a psychophysical event in which bodily disturbance becomes conscious under a meaningful form. The body supplies the affective material; consciousness, memory, and interpretation organize it as fear, grief, admiration, ecstasy, or depression.

French reception also matters. A French historical article notes that nineteenth-century emotion studies divided between physiological approaches and defenders of more metaphysical or behavior-centered views. In the French context, the James–Lange school was understood as defining emotion not as a prior mental state but as awareness of bodily manifestations following perception; François-Franck contested Lange’s French translation and tried to rehabilitate the role of the brain in triggering emotional impression.

Contemporary scholarship has also emphasized that Lange was marginalized partly because he became attached to James’s name and because later neurophysiology did not confirm his specific mechanism. Wassmann argues that a reappraisal is warranted because Lange helped ground emotion in physiology against spiritualist conceptions, contributed to biological psychiatry, postulated a neuroanatomical center, and anticipated later component or appraisal approaches.

This is the right balanced verdict: Lange’s specific vasomotor reduction is too narrow, but his general architecture—embodied affect, learned appraisal, memory, bodily mood, and symbolic mediation—is philosophically fertile.

12. Extracted philosophy of mind: systematic theses

A comprehensive Langean philosophy of mind can be reconstructed under ten theses.

1. Methodological anti-dualism. The mind should not be explained by positing a hidden soul-substance that produces bodily effects. Such an appeal often renames the problem rather than solving it. The burden is to explain how conscious affect arises in the organism.

2. Psychophysical unity. Mind and body are not two independent realms externally interacting. Lange’s affinity with Spinoza suggests that affect is one event with bodily and conscious aspects. The body is not a mere vehicle for mind; it is constitutive of affective consciousness.

3. Consciousness is not ontologically transparent. Introspection tells us that we feel fear, grief, joy, depression, or ecstasy. It does not by itself prove that these states originate as immaterial mental events. The felt distinction between “mental” and “physical” is not self-validating.

4. Emotion is embodied self-affection. An emotion is not first an inner psychic state and only afterward a bodily expression. It is the organism’s bodily modification becoming conscious in relation to an object, memory, situation, or symbolic frame.

5. Cognition routes affect rather than replacing physiology. Perception, belief, memory, and association are not excluded. They determine which bodily systems are activated, how strongly, for how long, and under what interpretation. A loaded gun, a religious hymn, and a love poem are affective because they are meaningful to a prepared organism.

6. Mood shows the autonomy of affect from reasons. Depression and anxiety can arise without adequate object or justification. Conscious affect need not be identical with belief, judgment, or rational appraisal.

7. Emotion categories are practical, not final. Common names such as anger, fear, sorrow, joy, admiration, and ecstasy are useful but conventional. Real affective life is continuous, graded, mixed, and physiologically complex.

8. Pleasure is a functional relation to sought states. Pleasure is not a simple mental atom. It is a condition or mood the organism seeks, sustains, repeats, or cultivates. This explains why painful content can become pleasurable in tragedy, religion, art, or disciplined performance.

9. Culture is embodied affect-training. Art, ritual, education, poetry, theater, and religion do not float above the body. They create pathways by which signs, memories, rhythms, doctrines, and practices mobilize bodily feeling. Culture educates the organism’s affective responsiveness.

10. The soul, if retained, is the lived psychophysical whole. Lange’s theory is hostile to the soul as an occult causal agent, but not to the seriousness of inner life. A Langean “soul” would mean the integrated living person: bodily, affective, mnemonic, conscious, symbolic, and socially formed.

13. Theological reconstruction: what becomes of the soul?

The theological implications are subtle.

Lange does not offer a theological anthropology in the full sense. He does not answer questions of immortality, sin, grace, divine image, or salvation. But his work strongly challenges any theology that treats the soul as an isolated inner substance whose passions can be understood apart from the body.

A theology informed by Lange would have to say that the soul is not spiritually pure interiority accidentally attached to flesh. Fear, repentance, consolation, ecstatic joy, hymn-singing, penitential sorrow, and mystical pain are embodied states. Religious life is not thereby debunked. Rather, it is located in the real human organism: memory, blood, nerves, posture, voice, ritual, imagination, doctrine, and community.

This is why his religious examples are so revealing. Ecstasy can be joy before divinity, terror before hell, compassion for Christ’s wounds, or bodily abnormality. The same religious tradition may cultivate both bliss and anguish. Lange’s theory does not decide whether the religious object is real; it explains how religious consciousness becomes affectively real in the person.

The resulting theological anthropology would be neither crude materialism nor disembodied spiritualism. It would be incarnational in a broad sense: the soul is known in embodied affective life. Even the most “spiritual” states are mediated by bodily processes, learned symbols, and communal practices.

14. Limits of Lange’s theory

The main limitation is the narrowness of the vasomotor mechanism. Lange himself partly recognized this. Near the end of The Emotions, he says he has opposed vasomotor function to other nervous activity partly for simplification, and that it may be only part of a broader involuntary nerve-muscle apparatus. That caveat matters. A defensible Langean philosophy of mind should not preserve the claim that vascular change alone explains emotion.

A second limit is that Lange sometimes underplays first-person meaning. Høffding’s criticism is powerful: one cannot derive sorrow, religious awe, or aesthetic admiration from physiology alone without subjective and interpretive context. A reconstructed Langean theory must therefore include phenomenology, language, and cultural meaning, not merely bodily mechanism.

A third limit is medical. His uric-acid account of periodical depression should not be adopted as contemporary psychiatry. Its philosophical importance lies in his recognition of bodily mood-generation and affect without adequate reason, not in the specific etiology.

A fourth limit is historical. Some of his remarks about civilization, education, and human groups reflect nineteenth-century assumptions that should be rejected. His general model of embodied regulation can be separated from those prejudicial frameworks.

15. Final synthesis

Lange’s philosophy of mind can be stated in one compact formula:

Conscious mental life is the organism’s bodily life as felt, remembered, interpreted, regulated, symbolized, and culturally educated.

This formula preserves the core of his theory while correcting its over-narrow physiology. Lange’s great insight is that the deepest regions of mind—emotion, mood, pleasure, aesthetic admiration, religious ecstasy, and even the apparent inwardness of the soul—cannot be understood apart from the body. But the body in Lange is not a mute machine. It is a remembering, learning, symbolically responsive organism.

His account therefore belongs neither to reductive materialism nor to classical spiritual dualism. It is a psychophysical philosophy of the person. The mind is not a ghost behind the body. The body is not a mere instrument of the mind. The “soul,” if the word is worth keeping, is the name for the living unity in which circulation, nerve, memory, language, art, worship, suffering, and consciousness become one human life.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
